data.title: Israel halts defense acquisitions from France
data.description:
Israel is stopping the acquisition of defense-related goods and services from France, the Israeli Defense Ministry said Tuesday.

“The Director General of the Israel Ministry of Defense. Maj. Gen. (Res.) Amir Baram has decided to reduce all defense procurement from France to zero, replacing it with domestic Israeli procurement or purchases from allied countries,” a Defense Ministry spokesperson said in a statement emailed to The Hill.

In the statement, the spokesperson said that “France has taken a series of actions that have harmed Israel’s security and the operational capabilities of its defense industry.”

“The Israel Ministry of Defense views the French government’s policy with serious concern, as it undermines security cooperation with Israel, a country that is actively operating on the front line against Iran and protecting the security of the Western world,” the ministry added later.

On Tuesday, President Trump went after France for being “very unhelpful” in the conflict against Iran following the European country not allowing planes on their way to Israel to fly over its territory.

“The Country of France wouldn’t let planes headed to Israel, loaded up with military supplies, fly over French territory,” Trump said on Truth Social. “France has been VERY UNHELPFUL with respect to the ‘Butcher of Iran,’ who has been successfully eliminated!”

“The U.S.A. will remember!!!” he wrote.

Earlier this month, French President Emmanuel Macron said his country “will never take part” in operations to open the Strait of Hormuz, which has been blocked amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict against Iran.

“We are not party to the conflict and therefore France ​will never take part in operations to open or liberate the Strait of Hormuz in the current context,” Macron said during a cabinet meeting, per Reuters.

data.title: Cory Booker calls for 'generational renewal' ahead of rumored 2028 bid
data.description:
Sen. Cory Booker looks like he’s gearing up for a presidential run, and he joined NBC’s “Meet the Press” on Sunday to openly critique his own party. The New Jersey Democrat didn’t hold back, telling anchor Kristen Welker that the party “has failed this moment” and needs a broader vision that moves past the entrenched left-versus-right divide.  

Welker: “Do you believe Democrats are making the mistake of shrinking their coalition with what you describe as purity tests, senator?”  
Booker: “Look, I’m proud of so many things that my Democratic colleagues are doing. But as a whole, our party has failed this moment.”  

Booker’s critique isn’t just political theater — it’s a reflection of a party still grappling with the lessons of the 2024 election. After Kamala Harris’s loss, Democrats face a critical choice: stick with the familiar, or make space for younger, bolder voices that can help rebuild a coalition. Booker argues the old playbook of constant partisan warfare has left the party over-focused on the last headline — and underprepared for the challenges ahead.  

“It’s why I’ve called for new leadership in America,” Booker said. “I’ve called for generational renewal because this left/right divide is killing our country. And our adversaries know it. They come onto our social media and try to whip up hate in America. That is one of our biggest crises. It is time for a new vision of our country that is far more uniting.”  

What stands out is Booker’s focus on forward-looking issues: AI, robotics and economic transformation. He’s right — these aren’t partisan flashpoints, they’re realities that will shape every voter’s life in the coming decade. Democrats risk alienating the very people they need if they remain tied to reactive politics rather than building a coalition based on solutions and unity. Booker’s call for generational renewal is more than a nod to younger voters, it’s a reminder that political longevity depends on relevance, not just resistance.  

Booker also cautioned against letting President Trump dominate the narrative, reminding most Americans that “we are not each other’s enemies.”  His point is sharp: focusing solely on opposing the former president doesn’t equip the party to lead on the issues that actually shape the future.  

For Democrats, the takeaway is clear. Embracing diverse voices and ideas isn’t optional; it’s essential. And it’s not just about winning elections — it’s about proving the party can deliver meaningful leadership that unites, rather than divides. Booker may be eyeing 2028, but his critique strikes at a deeper truth: the party that fails to evolve risks failing the country. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: How US allies in Europe are responding to the war in Iran
data.description:
Leaders across Europe are digging their heels in against military involvement in the Middle East, as President Trump repeatedly lashes out against NATO allies for their reluctance to support the U.S. operation in Iran.

The longstanding NATO alliance has come under increasing strain in recent months due to a deepening rift over the war with Iran, with the vital Strait of Hormuz at the epicenter of Trump’s pressure campaign.

Though some NATO countries, including the United Kingdom, have signaled a “readiness to contribute” to reopening the strait, they have resisted calls to enter the war in a broader capacity.

Trump has rebuffed their collective opposition, referring to NATO as a “paper tiger” and “cowards,” while issuing thinly veiled threats of economic consequences or to pull the U.S. out of the agreement entirely.

“You’ll have to start learning how to fight for yourself, the U.S.A. won’t be there to help you anymore, just like you weren’t there for us,” Trump wrote in a Truth Social post on Tuesday, referring to countries “which refused to get involved in the decapitation of Iran.”

United Kingdom

The United Kingdom has been singled out often by Trump since the joint U.S.-Israeli operation began on Feb. 28, with the president regularly lobbing criticism at British Prime Minister Keir Starmer over the country’s approach to the conflict.

Starmer blocked a request in late February for U.S. planes to use U.K. bases for preemptive strikes on Tehran over concerns it could violate international law.

That decision angered Trump, who used it to renew criticism of a deal between the U.K. and Mauritius on the Chagos Islands.

“This is not Winston Churchill that we’re dealing with,” Trump quipped to reporters in the White House a few days later.

Shortly after Iran launched its retaliatory campaign, Starmer reversed course. On March 1, he approved the use of the RAF Fairford and Diego Garcia bases on the Chagos Islands for “defensive” strikes as part of the U.S. military operation.

The shift did little to appease Trump, who rejected the U.K.’s offer to send two aircraft carriers to the region in the early days of the conflict.

“That’s OK, Prime Minister Starmer, we don’t need them any longer — But we will remember. We don’t need people that join Wars after we’ve already won!” Trump wrote on March 7 on Truth Social.

A March 20 decision expanded authorization for the U.S. to use British military bases to carry out strikes against Iranian missile sites being used to target ships in the Strait of Hormuz following escalating attacks on vessels and oil and gas facilities in the region.  

A Downing Street spokesperson noted at the time that the U.K. was “working closely” with international partners to develop a plan to safeguard commercial shipping from “reckless” Iranian strikes but affirmed the country’s position on the war was unchanged.

“The UK remains committed to defending our people, our interests and our allies, acting in accordance with international law and not getting drawn into the wider conflict,” the statement said.

The U.K. has boosted its military assets in the region in recent weeks as Iran steps up its aerial assault on U.S., Israeli and European interests in the Middle East.

The BBC reported on Tuesday that additional British troops and air defense systems were being deployed to the region to join UK Typhoon jets already stationed in Qatar.

The tensions between the U.S. and Britain could overshadow a visit from King Charles III and Queen Camilla scheduled for late April to commemorate America’s 250th birthday.  

France

France has urged a diplomatic solution since the outset of the conflict, joining the U.K. and Germany in pressing the U.S. to pursue negotiations with Iran on the day the joint military operation launched.

French President Emmanuel Macron condemned the initial strikes that killed former Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei as being conducted “outside of international law,” but also blamed Tehran for regional escalation.

Still, he has remained defiant that his country will not participate in the conflict, vowing on March 17 that France “will never take part” in operations to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

A day later, he called for a moratorium on U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iranian energy and water infrastructure.  

France came in Trump’s crosshairs on Tuesday, when the president criticized the country for being “very unhelpful” amid reports that it would not allow planes headed to Israel to fly over its territory. Sources told Reuters that Israel wanted to use France’s airspace to transport U.S. weapons to be used in the war.

“The U.S.A. will remember!!!” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

Italy

Italy has also refused to grant the U.S. permission to land military planes at an air base in Sicily before departing to the Middle East, according to Italian media reports.

Corriere della Sera reported on Tuesday that “several US bombers” planned to land at Sigonella air base on their way to the Middle East earlier this week but were denied because the U.S. had not requested authorization or consulted with Italian military leaders.

“With reference to press reports regarding the use of military bases, it is reiterated that Italy acts in full compliance with existing international agreements and with the policy guidelines expressed by the Government to Parliament,” a statement from Palazzo Chigi, the office of Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, said.

The government noted that all requests are examined “on a case-by-case basis” and said there were “no critical issues or frictions with international partners,” describing the U.S.-Italy relationship as “solid and based on full and loyal cooperation.”

Italian Defense Minister Guido Crosetto denied any rift with the U.S. on Tuesday, dismissing the reports as “simply false, because the bases are active, in use, and nothing has changed.”

Crosetto said both sides were aware of the provisions outlined in the 1954 collective defensive treaty, which require parliamentary approval for the use of Italian airbases in combat missions.

“International agreements clearly regulate and distinguish what requires specific Government authorization (for which it has been decided to always involve Parliament), without which it is not possible to grant anything, and what is instead considered technically authorized because it is included in the agreements,” Crosetto added. “A minister only has to ensure they are respected. There is no third option.”

Spain

Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez has perhaps been the most vocal opponent of the conflict with Iran, repeatedly warning about the potential toll on human life and economic disruption as strikes escalate.

“We must learn from history, we cannot play Russian roulette with the fate of millions of people,” Sánchez said on March 4, according to a transcript from the Secretariat of State for Communication.

“The powers involved in this conflict must immediately cease hostilities and choose dialogue and diplomacy. And the rest of us must act with coherence, defending now the same values that we defend when we talk about Ukraine, Gaza, Venezuela, or Greenland.”

The level of cooperation between the U.S. and Spain became the subject of debate early in the war.

Earlier this month, Trump threatened to “cut off all trade” with Madrid after its government blocked the U.S. from using the country’s jointly operated bases for strikes on Tehran not included in the United Nations charter.

The White House later claimed that Spain heeded Trump’s warning and changed its mind, which Spanish Foreign Minister José Manuel Albares “categorically” denied.

“The Spanish government’s position on the war in the Middle East, the bombings in Iran, and the use of our bases has not changed one iota,” Albares told Cadena SER radio on March 4.

Spain has now also closed its airspace to American aircraft involved in the military operation, deeming the ongoing campaign a violation of international law.

The U.S. has relocated 15 aircraft from the Rota and Morón military bases in the southern part of the country since the start of the month.

Germany

German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier described the ongoing conflict as a “disastrous mistake” in a speech at the German Foreign Ministry last Tuesday, criticizing the Trump administration’s reasoning.

“There is little doubt that, in any case, the justification of an imminent attack on the U.S. does not hold water,” Steinmeier said, according to Politico EU. 

German Chancellor Friedrich Merz has also expressed concerns about the U.S.-Israeli operation, questioning the rationale behind waging the war and whether there is a solid exit strategy.

“To this day, there is no convincing plan for how this operation could succeed,” Merz told German legislators on March 18, Reuters reported.

“Washington has not consulted us and did not say European assistance was necessary. … We would have advised against pursuing this course of action as it has been pursued.

Trump had previously taken a softer tone toward Germany, telling reporters during a meeting with Merz on March 3 that the European nation’s response had “been great.”

Since then, Germany has rejected calls to send warships to the Middle East to help secure safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

Ireland

Ireland’s government has worked to strike a balance between condemnation for the war and maintaining its commitment to neutrality, emphasizing the economic instability caused by the Middle East conflict.  

Still, Ireland’s Taoiseach Micheál Martin said ahead of a meeting with Trump at the White House on St. Patrick’s Day that Ireland would not participate in U.S. efforts to secure the Strait of Hormuz because it lacked the offensive military capacity to do so.

He reiterated that Ireland prefers a “peaceful resolution” to the war but noted that it “works both ways,” accusing Iran of inflaming tensions, according to The Independent.

Ireland is set to play a larger role on the world stage when it assumes the rotating presidency of the Council of the European Union in July, a position at the core of the bloc’s decision-making.

data.title: Dem Abdul El-Sayed afraid to offend Muslim ayatollah sympathizers?
data.description:
Abdul El-Sayed wants to be Michigan’s next senator, and is running in the Democratic primary. Of the three people in the race, he is the furthest left, and he knows it. And he’s apparently very cognizant of who his base is, and the risk of offending them. In fact, during a call with campaign staff, he told them that he wanted to steer clear of saying anything positive about U.S. forces taking out the ayatollah, because he was worried about offending Arab Muslims. 

This information comes from The Washington Free Beacon, which obtained audio of El-Sayed making these remarks.  

El-Sayed said he would rather not discuss news of Khamenei’s death at all, because there were a lot of people in Dearborn who were sad about it. Dearborn is an Arab-majority city near Detroit that voted for Donald Trump in the 2024 election, mainly due to frustrations with Joe Biden’s policy toward Gaza. Listen to what El-Sayed said specifically. 

And look, if he feels that way — if he thinks his base is Arab Muslims who sympathize with an extremist and terrorist regime — then yeah, he probably shouldn’t say anything about it. Frankly, what I found more disgusting than those remarks was that he planned to avoid discussing the ayatollah’s death by immediately pivoting to the Epstein scandal. If asked about Iran, he planned to call Trump a pedophile and say the war is a distraction from that. Listen to this.  

Let us be abundantly clear: What this man, Abdul El-Sayed, is accusing Donald Trump of is a vicious smear and a lie. There is no evidence whatsoever that Trump was complicit in the crimes of Jeffrey Epstein. There is no evidence whatsoever that Trump abused underage girls. Anyone who utters these lies should be disqualified from higher office, because there is clearly no depth they would avoid sinking to. It is intellectual lazy, it is morally destructive, and it is corrosive to our democracy, to say the other side is not just wrong, not just misguided, but is complicit in the rape of children. 

And it shows that Democrats and the mainstream media, who made so much hay out of saying that MAGA conservatives are insane for embracing conspiracy theories like Pizzagate, have proven themselves to be little better. Because at this point if you believe that Trump was on Epstein island, you are believing in conspiracy theories, too. 

Pizzagate is a fringe belief on the right, but for Democrats the Epstein-Trump version of this story is going mainstream. Just look at what Ro Khanna is doing. And look what El-Sayed aspires to do: distract the public, convince them to ignore his own moral cowardice, by fixating on a lie.  

As a former resident of the state of Michigan and the great city of Detroit, I am really hoping the people there don’t fall for it. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: US citizens in Saudi Arabia urged to shelter in place
data.description:
The State Department on Tuesday warned U.S. citizens in Saudi Arabia to shelter in place over potential threats from Iran amid the conflict in the region.

“We are tracking reports of threats against locations where American citizens gather.  We advise U.S. citizens that hotels and other gathering points including U.S. businesses and U.S. educational institutions may be potential targets,” the State Department’s Travel Gov wrote in a post on the social media platform X.

“The U.S. Embassy in Riyadh has directed all U.S. government employees to shelter in place.  We recommend all Americans in Saudi Arabia do the same until further notice,” the post continues.
“To the extent possible, remain in your residence or another structure, and stay away from windows.

On Sunday, Iran threatened to target U.S. satellite campuses in the Middle East after strikes hit Isfahan University of Technology and Tehran University of Science and Technology. Several buildings were damaged and four staff members were wounded in the attack.

Iran warned of retaliation and advised all U.S. students, professors and corresponding staff to stay at least a kilometer away from campuses in the region.

New York University, Georgetown University, Carnegie Mellon University, Northwestern University, Texas A&M University and other American schools have satellite campuses in regional countries such as Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia. 

Last week, an Iranian strike on Prince Sultan Air Base in Al-Kharj in Saudi Arabia injured 10 U.S. service members and damaged a $300 million Boeing-made E-3 Sentry, which serves as a valuable early warning and control aircraft.

Early in the war, Iran also attacked the U.S. Embassy in Riyadh leading the State Department to direct government employees to shelter in place. U.S. citizens are now being urged to depart.

“We encourage U.S. citizens to depart Saudi Arabia via commercial flights if you can do so safely.  audi airspace remains open with frequent air traffic restrictions to address continued missile and drone threats,” the State Department wrote in a Tuesday security alert. 

“Riyadh, Jeddah, and Dammam airports remain open and operational, but travelers are advised to check their flight status directly with the airline due to possible delays and cancellations,” it added.

Amid repeated strikes on U.S. assets in Saudi Arabia, Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) encouraged the country to join the war earlier this month.

data.title: 'Affordability' will be 'growing challenge' for Republicans in midterms: Marc Short
data.description:
Former White House Director of Legislative Affairs Marc Short on Monday said affordability will become a “growing challenge” for Republicans in midterm elections as costs rise amid the conflict with Iran.

“The reality is that prices are going to continue to go up. This is going to continue to ripple through. And I agree with Ana. It’s to say this is a short-term pain really sets an expectation for a lot of Americans that if it’s not, it’s going to create a bigger and bigger problem come November,” Short said during a Monday appearance on CNN’s “News Night.”

“And so, I do think that affordability was one of the primary issues that he was elected on,” Short said of President Trump. “I think it’s going to be a growing, growing challenge for Republicans in the midterms this November,” he added.

Big Spring Sale

  • The top deals of the Amazon Big Spring Sale
  • Clearance sales under $50
  • Stock up on everyday essentials

BestReviews is reader-supported and may earn an affiliate commission.

Republicans in Congress are aiming to keep their razor-thin majority in both chambers, but they face a challenge in the House.

The president’s party typically loses seats in a midterm election, and just a handful of gains by Democrats would be enough to flip the chamber.

Democrats have a tougher hill to climb to retake the Senate majority given the map. They would have to gain four Senate seats to take a 51-49 majority.

In February, strategist Karl Rove said Trump is “making the same mistake” former President Biden made when he insisted that his economic policies were putting more money in the pockets of Americans.

Amid criticism, the president has touted his impact on the economy and the initial impact of tariffs, although they were struck down by the Supreme Court.

Earlier this year, Trump predicted that 2026 will be “even better” than his first year back in office.

“There are factories and plants and thousands of businesses being built all over the country,” he said. “I just spoke to President Xi [Jinping] of China. Nobody has ever seen anything like what’s happening right now in America.”

The president is set to meet with Xi during a meeting next month to re-hash trade agreements with Beijing.

data.title: Feds investigate Fairfax County Public Schools over undocumented immigrant charged with groping girls
data.description:
The Education Department announced Tuesday it is investigating Fairfax County Public Schools over reports that an undocumented immigrant sexually assaulted 12 female students there. 

The Fairfax Police Department said Israel Flores Ortiz, an 18-year-old student, turned himself in on March 7 and has been charged with 13 counts of assault and battery.

The victims said Ortiz groped them from the back and front and stuck his hand in between the girl’s legs, local outlet 7News reported.

“There’s a group of about 12 individuals that have reported this assault,” one mother told the local outlet. “It was all perpetrated by a single individual who is a stranger to the girls. He just sneakily walked up behind them and put his hand in between their legs. It was not just a butt smack or a butt grab. It was a groping of a private area. It had been occurring for several months.” 

Ortiz is being held on no bond and has a court appearance in April.  

Fairfax County Public Schools said it will cooperate with the Education Department and has requested an outside independent law firm to review the matter.  

“We are grateful to our law enforcement partners and are continuing to work closely with them as their investigation into this matter moves forward. To maintain the integrity of the ongoing investigation, we cannot share additional details at this point,” the district said in a statement.  

“In addition to the law enforcement investigation, Superintendent Dr. Michelle Reid has requested that FCPS retain an independent outside law firm to conduct a comprehensive review of this matter. The goal is clear: to establish a definitive understanding of what occurred, when it occurred, and confirm that all policies, procedures, and regulations were properly followed. The firm of McGuireWoods will conduct this thorough, objective, and independent review,” the district added. 

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) said Ortiz came to the U.S. illegally from El Salvador in 2024, FOX5 reported.  

“We are calling on Fairfax County to honor our detainer to ensure this violent criminal is removed from our country so he can never claim another victim again,” DHS reportedly said.  

The Hill has reached out to DHS for comment.  

The Education Department said in its statement this is just the latest in investigations that have been opened up against schools in Northern Virginia.  

“Virginia’s experiment with radical gender ideology, race-based admissions systems, far-Left indoctrination, and unlawful discrimination has inflicted immeasurable harm on our students. It must stop,” the department wrote.  

It pointed to the investigation it opened into the five Northern Virginia school districts that allow transgender women into female locker and bathrooms. 

“Female students say they witnessed male students inappropriately touch other students and watch female students change in a female locker room. Many female students in the Divisions have reported that they avoid using the restroom at school because the policies make them feel unsafe and uncomfortable,” the department said.  

The Trump administration has gone after numerous states, universities and K-12 districts that allow transgender women on female sports teams and in female bathrooms.

data.title: Judge halts White House ballroom construction project
data.description:
A federal judge on Tuesday halted construction on President Trump’s White House ballroom project.

U.S. District Judge Richard Leon said that no statute “comes close” to granting Trump the authority he claims he has to execute the $400 million project, barring construction from continuing until Congress authorizes its completion.

“The President of the United States is the steward of the White House for future generations of First Families,” Leon wrote. “He is not, however, the owner!”

DEVELOPING

data.title: Judge rules Trump administration unlawfully terminated legal status of migrants who used Biden-era app
data.description:
A federal judge in Massachusetts unwound the Trump administration’s termination of parole status for some 900,000 migrants who were given permission to temporarily live in the United States.

The Biden administration launched the CBP One app to address high flows at the border, allowing migrants to make an appointment to seek asylum, while others were “paroled” into the country for up to two years.

U.S. District Court Judge Allison Burroughs, an Obama appointee, found that the Trump administration violated the law last April when it emailed the group en masse telling them, “It is time for you to leave the United States.”

“The regulations do not give the agency unfettered discretion to terminate parole,” Burroughs wrote.

“When Defendants terminated the impacted noncitizens’ parole without observing the process mandated by statute and by their own regulations, they took action that was ‘not in accordance with law.’”

The Venezuelan Association of Massachusetts, one of the plaintiffs in the case, said the ruling “brings long-awaited relief after months of fear and uncertainty.”

Democracy Forward, which helped litigate the case, also celebrated the ruling.

“Today’s ruling is a clear rejection of an administration that has tried to erase lawful status for hundreds of thousands of people with the click of a button,” Skye Perryman, the group’s president, said in a ruling. 

“Our clients followed the law: they waited, registered, were inspected, and were granted parole under the law. The Trump-Vance administration’s effort to tear that status away overnight was unlawful and cruel — and today, the court rejected that harmful and destabilizing policy.”

The Trump administration had argued that President Biden abused parole authority by broadly awarding the status, rather than awarding it on a case-by-case basis.

President Trump shut down the app during his first month in office.

data.title: US trade rep says he sees 'positive agenda with China going forward'
data.description:
U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said Tuesday that he envisions a “positive agenda” on trade with China moving forward, amid a years-long tariff battle between the two countries. 

“The Chinese want stability. We want stability,” Greer told host Annmarie Hordern on “Bloomberg Surveillance.” “I actually see a positive agenda with China going forward where we learn to manage our trade with each other, where we pick the kinds of things we want to be selling to each other, things that are mostly not sensitive to avoid some of the national security elements that prove challenging in negotiations.”

The trade representative also noted that he sees this “stability” with China “over the next year.”

President Trump is set to meet with Chinese President Xi Jinping on May 14 and 15 in Beijing, a summit that was pushed back due to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran. Trump also said earlier this month that Xi will visit the U.S. in the “not too distant future.”

Greer noted Tuesday that he does not envision meeting with his Chinese counterparts before the meeting between Trump and Xi.

Last month, the Supreme Court struck down the president’s tariffs imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, including those on Chinese imports. In issuing levies on goods from China and dozens of other nations last year, Trump declared national emergencies related to the trafficking of fentanyl and the U.S. trade deficit. 

After the high court’s ruling, Trump imposed 10 percent tariffs on imports from countries around the world under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, which allows for tariffs of up to 15 percent to address “large and serious” trade deficits for no more than 150 days. Congress would need to approve an extension after the 150-day period ends on July 24.

Greer, meanwhile, opened probes earlier this month into the tax policies and practices of numerous foreign partners, including China, the European Union, Japan, Mexico and India, under Section 301 of the Trade Act. That clause allows the president to implement tariffs in response to “unjustifiable,” “unreasonable” or discriminatory” trade practices by other countries.

In response, the Chinese Commerce Ministry launched two investigations into U.S. trade practices last week, according to The Associated Press.

On Tuesday, Greer said he cannot “prejudge” the investigations into unfair trade practices, but vowed that the administration is prioritizing American firms “so we can continue to have increased production in the U.S. [and] increased wages.”
